evidence_quotes.quote: The most affected by computer games.
the seller reserves the right to disable/withdraw/degrade it.

paragraphs.text: Dear Commission sees the right step in protecting consumer rights. And having regard to the information provided (https://www.digital-fairness-act.com/), what will be affected by this legislation. I would very much like to make an additional recommendation to cover another of the ever-increasing online scourges. This concerns the sale of virtual goods. In short, the item is being bought, but the seller reserves the right to disable/withdraw/degrade it. Below, I have made further efforts to present arguments/examples. I am just an ordinary citizen, so the information can only appear superficial. Thank you for your time! When an e-book is purchased, the seller reserves the right to withdraw the item sold. The item is sold as physical, but it is deeply hidden between the legal jargon that the purchase is a rental (Amazon as an example https://www.zdnet.com/article/why-amazon-is-within-its-rights-to-remove-access-to-your-kindle-books/) and the seller allows itself to change the content of the book after the purchase, thus changing the purchase itself. This is also the case with the films https://theoutline.com/post/6167/apple-can-delete-the-movies-you-purchased-without-telling-you The most affected by computer games. Physical copies are no longer in principle retained. When buying a ‘physical’ copy, a game key is obtained, which can only be ‘purified’ by means of an online distribution platform (Steam, GOG, Epic Store). All games can be easily renovated, errors are corrected, but it also gives the publisher the right to damage the game without the possibility to continue playing the old version. The citizen ‘purchases’ the game with an inoperative software file. There are over 350 games (non-exhaustive list) that have become completely inoperative after some time, as the developer has not left the possibility for the players themselves to hand over the server hosting https://stopkillinggames.wiki.gg/wiki/Dead_game_list The ‘Stop killing games’ movement has emerged around this sensitive problem, as consumer rights usually protect computer games/consoles. The movement itself has already collected over 1.4 million signatures in the citizens’ initiative. In conclusion, I would very much like to ask for the new act to cover virtual goods whose purchase would not be covered by the word ‘buy’ but rather the word ‘lease’. This would reflect the temporary nature of the product, eliminate misunderstandings and correctly inform the consumer until when he can expect his purchase to function (possibly used) For your attention, Ignas Lapėnas
